The surgeon performs a laparoscopic cholecystectomy on a 46-year-old female patient with post-gallstone pancreatitis. The procedure involves using an extra port due to the level 2A difficulty. Electrocautery is employed for dissection, and clips are applied. A drain is placed at the end of the procedure.

On table, we have decided we would like to go for laparoscopic cholecystectomy instead of any conversion. So there is higher chance of finishing it up with laparoscopic cholecystectomy. And beforehand, we can know this. So let's go to a video discussion.

The video displays the liver with visible adhesions and the falciform ligament. The surgeon uses a telescope port for visualization and performs transfacial insertion of the epigastric port, navigating through the falciform ligament to reach the right hypochondrium area where the gallbladder is located.

The surgeon uses a Maryland Dissector through the epigastric port to remove intra-abdominal adhesions around the gallbladder. The laparoscope is initially inserted through the umbilical port but is later repositioned through the epigastric port for better visualization. The surgeon checks and adjusts the positioning of the umbilical port under telescopic vision to ensure proper access and visibility.

The surgeon uses traction to manipulate the gallbladder and surrounding tissues, avoiding electrocautery to prevent damage to vital structures. The laparoscope is cleaned for improved visualization, revealing the gallbladder, stomach, greater omentum, and liver. Multiple ports are inserted, including a new 5mm port, amidst extensive adhesions from post-pancreatitis. The surgeon continues to clear adhesions using mechanical force.

The surgeon uses a Maryland Dissector and an Atraumatic Grasper to carefully strip adhesions from the gallbladder, which is adhered to the greater omentum, colon, and duodenum. The assistant holds the gallbladder fundus to provide traction towards the patient's right shoulder. The gallbladder wall appears thin, and the surgeon avoids using electrosurgery, relying on mechanical traction and dissection. The Calot's triangle and posterior fold are not initially visible due to excessive fat and adhesions.

Electrosurgery is used to carefully dissect the gallbladder from surrounding adhesions, avoiding traction that could tear the liver capsule and cause bleeding. The surgeon uses a suction cannula with a 5 mm reducer through the epigastric port, while the umbilical port serves as the telescopic view. Multiple ports are utilized, including three 10 mm ports and two 5 mm ports. Despite extensive adhesions, the reverse sulcus is not visible due to fusion, and gauze is used for pressure application during suction.

The surgeon uses a Maryland Dissector and atraumatic grasper to dissect around the cystic duct, attempting to create a plane for clearer visualization. The gallbladder outline is visible, but the Ruvia sulcus is not observed. Suction is applied to manage slight bleeding during the procedure.
